summary:
Abu Hureyra was a large prehistoric settlement in the Euphrates valley of northern Syria, occupied in two main phases spanning approximately 11,500 to 7,000 BCE. Like Mureybet, the site now lies beneath Lake Assad following the construction of the Tabqa Dam, but was excavated between 1972 and 1973 in an emergency salvage project before flooding. The resulting data from Abu Hureyra constitute one of the most detailed and informative records of the Neolithic transition from foraging to farming anywhere in the world.

The first occupation (Abu Hureyra 1), dating to approximately 11,500–10,000 BCE, represents a large and relatively sedentary hunter-gatherer community of the Epipalaeolithic period. The inhabitants intensively harvested an extraordinary range of wild plants — over 150 species have been identified from the charred botanical remains — and hunted Persian gazelle in seasonal drives. Analysis of the skeletal remains of women from this phase reveals severe degenerative conditions in their toes, knees, and lower back consistent with prolonged kneeling to process grain using saddle querns, providing direct physical evidence of the labor-intensive nature of wild plant food processing.

Abruptly, around 10,800 BCE, the site appears to have been briefly abandoned, likely in response to the [[event:1261|Younger Dryas climatic downturn]], which reduced the availability of wild foods. When re-occupied (Abu Hureyra 2), the community had adopted fully agricultural practices — cultivating rye, emmer wheat, and einkorn — making Abu Hureyra one of the earliest known sites of systematic crop cultivation in the world. The site grew substantially in this second phase, eventually covering around 11.5 hectares with a substantial population living in mud-brick structures.

Abu Hureyra demonstrates that the transition to agriculture was not a gradual inevitable progression but could be driven by environmental stress, and that once initiated, farming communities could grow rapidly in size and complexity.
